The smallest Pycnopodia helianthoides (Sunflower Sea Star) I've ever seen, 7cm across its body, 13 arms. Great news since they're almost extinct after Sea Star wasting. This is the second juvenile I've seen there in a month, after seeing none anywhere for two years. This one was floating upside down in shallow water during the -3.5; I placed it briefly in the plastic tub for a photo, never touching the tube feet, and released it into the same place.

description: A Pycnopodia helianthoides with Sea Star Wasting Syndrome. On Oct. 9, 2013 there were still thousands of these stars in the shallows of Indian Arm (second image), although many were starting to die. By Oct. 29, 2013 not a single living Pycnopodia could be found (third image). The remains of the stars were found in rotting heaps at the base of the rocks (fourth image).
place_guess: Croker Island, Indian Arm, BC, Canada
tag_list: British Columbia, Indian Arm, Sea Star Wasting Syndrome

description: Prior to October 2013 there were thousands of these sea stars in the shallows of Indian Arm. Almost all of them died within a few weeks during the devastating Sea Star Wasting Syndrome die-off in October 2013. Since then only a few have been found. On this day three were observed but this one had Sea Star Wasting Syndrome.
place_guess: Croker Island, Indian Arm, BC, Canada
tag_list: British Columbia, Indian Arm, Sea Star Wasting Syndrome

description: In September 2013 thousands of Pycnopodia helianthoides were found dead and dying in Howe Sound, BC. Within a few weeks almost every one was gone from the shallow subtidal zone where they had once been very abundant. The third image was taken on Dec 1, 2010 and shows the very high densities (up to 12 per square metre) of these large stars which were common prior to Sept. 2013.

description: The terror of the tide pools, sunflower stars eat anything and everything. This one was moving towards a live dungeness crab as we watched it, even though starfish do not feed out of water. Starfish feed by pushing out their stomachs and digesting their food without ever having to in-take it. Thanks to my friend Celso Reyes for taking photos when my camera was out.

description: I'd been going to tide pools for over 2 years and had never even heard of these and honestly it freaked me out when I saw it. Thought it was so crazily mutated ochre star. Glad to see it's actually a different kind of sea star. It was on the run too. I'd never seen a sea star move like that. Here's a video: http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=FRnJDwsBcHA
As usual, very common at Pigeon, though I didn't sea any of the bright red morph.  It was cool seeing these in the wild and then seeing them later in the aquarium.
